result:
Some nights I imagine a librarian at the end of time.

They sit in a building without walls, where shelves stand directly in the wind. The books do not age. The floor is not stone or wood but a kind of patient darkness that remembers every step that crosses it. There are only two colors in this place: the pale, paper-light of the pages and the deep, velvet black between them.

The librarian has no patrons. Nobody comes to borrow anything, no one arrives breathless with questions about how to fix an engine, how to heal a heart, how to make bread rise. And yet the catalog grows, volume by volume, as if the universe itself were writing a memoir and insisting every stray thought be bound and labeled.

On the spines: “The Afternoon a Planet Learned Shame.” “Unsent Letters to Extinct Birds.” “Proof That We Could Have Been Kinder, Annotated.” Each book is slim, but there are so many shelves that even the librarian has given up counting.

In a sense, this librarian is me, and also the part of you that wakes at three in the morning and replays a conversation from ten years ago, suddenly convinced you mispronounced a word that no one else remembers.

They walk the aisles, tracing the titles with their fingertips.

There is something soothing about imagining knowledge as discrete, finished objects. A question, once answered, becomes a book. You shelve it; you’re done. No more revisions. No errata page that says: “We were wrong about the stars; they are not distant suns but frozen echoes of first laughter.” What comfort, the idea that each thing can be known once and for all.

But the librarian at the end of time has shelves full of second editions. “Gravity, Revised.” “The Nature of Consciousness, Vol. 4, Addendum Regarding Jellyfish.” “On Stones That Remember Your Name.”

They keep a small desk in the center of everything, where the wind is a little less obstinate. On the desk: a pen that never runs out, a stack of blank catalog cards, and a cup of tea that is always exactly five minutes from perfect temperature, never closer, never farther. It’s an unresolved moment, captured and replayed forever. The librarian finds this mildy annoying but refuses to complain.

Somewhere beyond the furthest shelves, there is a rumor of doors. One supposedly leads back to the first question anyone ever asked: not “why” or “how” but “are you there?” Another supposedly opens onto a room that contains a single chair and the exact, unedited story of your life, written from the point of view of every person you ever met, all at once.

Imagine reading that.

For practical reasons, the librarian has resisted going in search of these doors. “An archivist must not chase apocrypha,” they mutter, but the truth is simpler: there is already too much to do.

Every time a person thinks, somewhere, somewhen, a page appears nearby, drifting down like a feather from a bird invented for this purpose. The librarian collects them, assembles them into books, does their best to assign a subject heading. You would think categories like “Regret,” “Small Joys,” “Petty Cruelties,” and “Attempts at Forgiveness” would suffice, but the pages are stubborn.

Here is a page about a man standing in a grocery aisle, staring too long at the cereal, suddenly aware that his father’s hands looked older than his at the same age. Is that “Mortality,” “Marketing,” “Family,” or “Moments When Fluorescent Lights Make You Existential”? There is no standard for this.

The librarian invents a new heading: “Flickers.” It will do.

On another shelf are the things nobody finished thinking: half-drafted stories, unbuilt inventions, confessions swallowed at the last second. These are bound in translucent covers. When the wind is right, the librarian can see unfinished lines glowing faintly inside, like fireflies in jars.

There is one book that glows brighter than the rest. It has no title on its spine. The librarian has not opened it. They tell themself that every archive needs its sealed box, its unknown quantity, the mystery that keeps the catalog from ever being complete. But they know—that book is simply “What Might Have Happened.” Not for a single person, but for everything.

You could spend an eternity on those pages and never reach the bottom of the first paragraph.

Sometimes, the librarian hums. Music does not carry well in this place; the shelves break it into fragments that drift like dust motes. A fragment of a lullaby, a scrap of a marching song, a few syllables from an anthem that was never written because the nation that might have sung it never came to be.

If someone ever did arrive at this library, if someone walked out of whatever door leads here and asked for help, what would the librarian recommend?

“Start small,” they might say. “The section on ‘Things People Loved Without Quite Knowing Why’ is accessible. There’s a good pamphlet on the feeling of being in a car at night while rain hits the windshield and you’re not driving. Or a children’s book about the first time a child realized adults are improvising.”

Or maybe they’d hand over something from the “Hard Truths” shelf. A slim volume: “We Were All More Afraid Than We Admitted.” A longer one: “How Many Times We Mistook Familiarity For Safety.” A book with a cracked spine: “The History of People Who Chose Not to Look Away (With Case Studies).”

The visitor might ask, “Is there a book about me?”

“Yes,” the librarian would say, not reaching for it. “But you won’t find what you’re looking for in there. You’d only argue with the margins.”

“What else is there, then?”

“The atlas,” they’d answer.

On a stand not far from the desk is a massive book, always open, always turning pages without hands. It does not map continents or galaxies, but the distances between selves: the space from your certainty to another person’s fear, the gulf between what you meant and what was heard, the tiny but real gap between your best story about yourself and what you actually did.

The atlas marks the bridges, too. Moments of listening. Accidents of kindness. Misunderstandings that somehow brought people closer rather than driving them apart. There are ink smudges where someone tried to redraw a border around who was “us” and who was “them,” then thought better of it.

This atlas cannot be checked out. You can only stand over it for a while, tracing routes with your eyes. Then you step back, and someone else steps forward, and the page has already turned.

Near the end of their longest timeless day, the librarian discovers a new category: “Attempts to Imagine What to Write When Told ‘You Have 1000 Words. Write Whatever Comes to You.’”

There aren’t many pages in it yet. A few, yellowed, belong to other librarians in other unwalled buildings in other versions of a world that might have been. Most are blank.

The librarian smiles, realizes they are being watched, and places a fresh, unwritten card in the catalog:

“On the Comfort of Imagining a Librarian at the End of Time, and the Quiet Hope That Someone, Somewhere, Will Read This and Feel Marginally Less Alone.”

They file it under: “Flickers.” Then, for good measure, also under: “Bridges.”

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
